data.title: Ternium Hit Hard By Trade-Driven Market Issues, But Not Out Of The Game
data.description:
The last year or so has been a rough one for Ternium (TX), as this high-quality Mexican steel producer has been hit hard by trade-related issues in both its home country and Brazil (a major secondary market) on top of a general period of malaise in steel driven by weaker end-user demand.

Ternium shares are actually up since my last article on the company, with the share price up around 16% and a robust dividend boosting the total return to closer to 30%. While the share-price performance hasn’t been as good as what Arcelor Mittal (MT) and Steel Dynamics (STLD) have done, it compares pretty well with other comparables like CSN (SID), Gerdau (GGB), and Nucor (NUE).

I believe 2025 should be the trough for the cycle for Ternium, and a full-year EBITDA margin of 10% isn’t bad for a trough year in this industry, but there are still significant unknowns that will impact 2026 performance, including trade negotiations between the U.S. and Mexico and Brazil and foreign exporters of steel into that country. Assuming that Ternium can settle back into a more normal cyclical pattern, I do think these shares are undervalued below $50, but there's above-average risk here, particularly given that many key drivers are out of management’s control or influence.

Mexico’s Import Sector Is Basically “On Pause”

The large majority of Ternium’s steel sold in Mexico goes toward manufactured exports (cars in particular) and infrastructure (construction and other supporting products), and neither are in great shape this year.

Although overall exports from Mexico to the U.S. are still growing, and growing surprisingly well, auto production has declined almost 1% year-to-date, and other metrics like industrial production have been down virtually the entire year (down about 2% year-to-date through September), and gross fixed investment has fallen for 13 straight quarters, with construction down more than 10% and non-residential construction down more than 16%.

All of that has, in turn, driven a roughly 10% year-to-date decline in apparent steel demand, and there’s not much any steel company can do when demand falls off that much. Third quarter revenue in Mexico fell about 13% yoy and slightly from the prior quarter, driven by a nearly 15% yoy decline in volumes. Careful expense control has allowed the company to post EBITDA growth (from lower bases), but it’s not a healthy situation at present.

The extent to which this improves next year is still up for debate. Ternium management sounds confident that ongoing USMCA negotiations will lead to a positive resolution with respect to tariffs on imports from Mexico, but that very much remains to be seen – companies have not started relocating production out of Mexico to any large degree, and there are still sound economic reasons to source from Mexico, but we’re basically in uncharted territory where this is all concerned.

It doesn’t really make that much difference to Ternium whether the U.S. places tariffs on steel imported from Mexico. While the company has historically exported a small amount of its production to the U.S., it’s not a meaningful part of the business, and Ternium has never looked to change that – Ternium’s focus is on replacing imported steel within Mexico for applications like cars, machinery, and construction, as imported steel still has around 40%-50% share in Mexico (and closer to two-thirds share in the auto sector). Thus, it’s far more important to Ternium that there be positive resolutions with respect to auto and metal goods exported from Mexico into the United States.

A Different Sort Of Trade Issue Is Bedeviling The Brazilian Business

Brazil generates close to a quarter of Ternium’s revenue, and it’s an increasingly significant market for the company now that it has increased its ownership stake in Usiminas. Unfortunately, while Brazil’s economy is actually doing rather well at present, the Brazilian government’s policies on imported steel are depriving its local steelmakers from sharing in the upside.

Geopolitics Step to Center Stage

Despite almost 4% year-to-date growth in domestic auto production and growth in other areas of Brazil’s economy (including infrastructure), hot-rolled steel prices have fallen more than 20% due to a surge of imports. Steel imports are up about 9% year-to-date after much larger increases in prior years, with China leading the way (up 25% year-to-date and up about 361% since 2020), and local producers simply cannot compete on price with these imports.

Thus far Brazil’s government has done little. While there's a Tariff Rate Quota system in place, it faces severe challenges, as it exempts steel from free-trade zones and countries that have bilateral trade agreements with Brazil.

More pragmatically, the Brazilian government is loathe to antagonize China, and that’s understandable given that almost 30% of Brazil’s exports go to China now, and Brazil has been more than happy to step up with exports of soybeans, animal proteins, and other agricultural products in response to trade frictions between the U.S. and China.

Even though there are investigations underway on alleged anti-dumping cases that would pertain to about 75% of Usiminas' portfolio, I’m not getting my hopes up at this point. The Brazilian government already kicked the can down the road on a decision on cold-rolled and pre-painted imports from China that was supposed to be made in mid-November, and the ministry could wait as long as early 2027 before issuing rulings.

Maybe the Brazilian government is just hoping the problem goes away, but I’m not optimistic. China hasn’t been adhering to WTO rules for quite some time now, and in some respects they need to dump their steel on the global market – they produce more than half of the world’s steel (and it employs around 3.4M people), but they consume about 48% of global steel and that number is falling due to weakness in the property sector and companies shifting machinery production out of China.

Ternium Deepens Its Commitment To Brazil

Nevertheless, Ternium remains committed to this market. Early in November the company acquired the Usiminas stakes held by Nippon Steel and Mitsubishi for about $315M (paying more than double the market price because of pre-existing purchase agreements), taking their ownership from 51.5% to 83.1%, with the broader T/T Group now owning almost 93% of the company.

It’s unclear if Ternium triggered a buy clause or Nippon Steel triggered its put option, but judging by the commentary of Nippon Steel (saying they don’t expect a meaningful recovery in Brazil), I think they triggered the deal.

While this move will simplify management at Usiminas and accelerate decision making (there won’t be protracted discussions around strategy, capex, et al.), it does consume about half of Ternium’s net cash on hand, and Usiminas is going to need some capex reinvestment down the road (especially for its coke batteries and mining assets).

The Outlook

While cautious on the next 12 months, I do think it is more likely than not that Ternium is past the worst of the cycle. Still, a lot of the company’s rebound is tied to geopolitics at this point; I still believe in the long-term potential of Mexico as a key manufacturing and export hub, and I still believe in Ternium’s prospects for replacing imported steel within Mexico’s manufacturing and infrastructure sectors, but a lot rides on those trade negotiations.

Likewise with Brazil. I don’t think that the Brazilian government is willing to gut its domestic steel industry to appease China, but I do have real concerns that the government will do less (and perhaps far less) than it could in order to preserve its overall trade relationships with China.

With that, I’m not looking for a big rebound. This year is likely to end with Ternium’s revenue falling around 12% yoy, and I expect a modest mid-single-digit recovery in 2026 and 2027 and long-term growth in the low single-digits.

Ternium is a very efficient producer when volumes are healthy, and I believe management will meet, and likely exceed, its $150/ton target for steel EBITDA when volumes recover (the most recent quarter saw steel EBITDA of $104/ton; still a good number relative to many steel companies).

With that, I still expect long-term FCF margins to average out in the high single digits, supporting strong growth off of a low base. That said, FCF will likely be negative next year as well as the company wraps up a capex expansion program, and it will likely cap dividend growth in the near term.

Ternium looks meaningfully undervalued on discounted cash flow (with a fair value in the high-$50’s) provided the next ten years look at least something like the company’s long-term performance. While discounted cash flow is a tricky way to value cyclical companies like Ternium (where visibility beyond a year or two is minimal), I have noticed that buying in at a steep discount to assumptions predicated on returns to long-term averages works more often than not.

Turning to a multiples-based approach, a 4x multiple on blended year-ahead and full-cycle EBITDA gets me to a fair value of about $50.

The Bottom Line

Generally speaking it works out well if you buy well-run cyclical commodity companies in the throes of their downturns, and that would seem to be where Ternium is at now. Of course there are no guarantees that the future will resemble the past, but the need for steel isn’t going away, Ternium remains a highly efficient operator with attractive market/share growth potential, and the arguments for free global trade are still sound. It's likely going to take a while longer for Ternium’s numbers to recover, but I do think today’s price is attractive.

data.title: Praxis Precision Medicines: Latest Study Wins Sets Up 2 CNS Drug Approvals In 2026
data.description:
Investment Overview - Praxis Caps Off Incredible Year With Another Study Success

Praxis Precision Medicines, Inc. (PRAX) stock is soaring in trading today, up 35% at time of writing, with shares reaching a value of $260, their highest value since the beginning of 2022. Current market cap is ~$6.5bn.

The catalyst for gains appears to be news shared yesterday from Praxis' EMBOLD study, evaluating its candidate relutrigine for the treatment of patients with SCN2A and SCN8A developmental and epileptic encephalopathies ("DEEs") - according to a press release:

SCN2A and SCN8A DEEs are devastating conditions with extremely high mortality due to the debilitating seizure burden they impose on patients, and there are currently no approved treatment options.

Praxis appears not have shared any specific data, rather news that successful interim analysis of the study data has triggered "an early stop for efficacy." In other words, study investigators have declared it a success.

There was further good news to share in that Praxis confirmed that, following a meeting with the Food and Drug Administration ("FDA"), to discuss its path to a New Drug Application ("NDA") submission for another lead asset, ulixacaltamide, indicated for essential tremor, it had:

gained alignment from the agency on the content of the NDA and expects to complete its NDA submission in early 2026.

Praxis completed a $190m IPO at a price of ~$17 in October 2020, and was initially focused on candidate PRAX-114, indicated for major depressive disorder ("MDD"). The drug did not live up to expectations, however, and was dropped following a Phase 2/3 study fail.

Relutrigine is only one of three late stage assets in Praxis' "Cerebrum" small molecule platform pipeline (its second platform, "Solidus," develops antisense, oligonucleotide drug candidates), the others being vormatigine, and ulixacaltamide, as shown in the pipeline overview below (taken from an investor presentation).

In October, Praxis shared positive topline data from two Phase 3 studies of ulixacaltamide in essential tremor ("ET"), in which, according to a press release:

  • Patients treated with ulixacaltamide in the parallel-group study (Study 1) showed a mean improvement from baseline in the Modified Activities of Daily Living 11 at Week 8, the pre-specified primary endpoint, of 4.3 points (p<0.0001).
  • All key secondary endpoints in Study 1 - rate of disease improvement over 12 weeks, PGI-C and CGI-S - were also statistically significant (p<0.001).
  • Study 2 met its pre-specified primary endpoint, with patients showing superior maintenance of effect while on ulixacaltamide versus placebo during the randomized-withdrawal phase (p=0.0369).
  • The first key secondary endpoint in Study 2 - rate of disease improvement during the randomized-withdrawal phase - also demonstrated superior effect in ulixacaltamide treated patients versus placebo (p=0.0042).

On that news, Praxis stock jumped in value from ~$55 per share, to $190 per share, a gain of ~245%. All in all, then, it has been an excellent year for Praxis, with shares up 229% year-to-date, and up ~500% in a six months basis.

Can Praxis Capitalize On Study Wins & Bring Drugs To Market?

Praxis believes that relutrigine - a sodium channel modulator - addresses a market of ~200k patients, worth ~$3bn.

I have not yet discussed vormatrigine, also a sodium channel modulator, in four late stage studies, one of which - a Phase 2/3 - will read out data in focal onset seizures and generalized epilepsy, a market of 3.5m patients, worth >$2.5bn in value, in 1H 2026, management says.

Regarding relutrigine, Praxis says that its drug has "potential for class leading efficacy and tolerability." It is administered orally or via gastrojejunostomy ("G/J") tube, and it's mechanism of action ("MoA") is discussed below:

Data from Cohort 1 of the EMBOLD study demonstrated "sustained seizure reduction with continued exposure on top of standard-of-care" ("SOC"), with no drug related serious adverse treatment events, and improvements noted both in the clinical and daily life settings.

The EMERALD study of relutrigine is ongoing, with data due next year potentially opening up a larger patient population, but it seems Praxis will submit its NDA ahead of those results. Analysts have "conservatively" forecast peak revenues of $650m by 2037.

Meanwhile, management estimates ~7m people suffer from ET, which carries a "high psychosocial burden," and is "inadequately managed and undertreated." Apparently, ulixacaltamide works through "precision modulation of tremor circuits through T-type calcium channel modulation," and is the first drug to record a positive result in a Phase 3 study.

As we can see above, Praxis believes all of its hypotheses around the safety and efficacy of ulixacaltamide support approval - from a safety perspective, the company says that the most common (≥10%) adverse events in patients were constipation, dizziness, euphoric mood, brain fog, headache, paraesthesia and insomnia.

In fairness to Praxis, you cannot ask for much more than meeting all endpoints in two separate pivotal studies, plus positive feedback from the FDA, therefore, we may speculate the market is right to be sending Praxis stock skywards.

The company could submit two NDA's next year, with compelling study evidence (we don't know what the latest relutrigine data were, but we know they were good enough to halt the study early), one for a drug that may earn ~$650m in peak revenues and the second for a drug analysts have suggested could earn as much as ~$3bn per annum.

For good measure, there will be pivotal study results for vormatrigine, also, another drug that may have "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) sales potential. If approved, all three drugs would be awarded many years of patent protection, meaning premium price points can be applied. And, a final plus, data from its ASO platform, in the form of elsunersen in SCN2A GoF (Gain-of-Function) indications.

Analysis - Why I'd Expect More Price Volatility In 2026

Clearly, the optimal time to buy Praxis stock would have been six months ago, but do shares still present good value today?

I'm somewhat conflicted - central nervous system diseases are highly complex and patients perhaps react differently and subjectively to different drugs, making it hard to understand which drug candidates may succeed, and which won't.

I have used Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Cobenfy a few time in notes this year to illustrate my point. The combination therapy was guided through clinical studies by Karuna Therapeutics, who achieved a resounding success in schizophrenia, prompting BMY to acquire Karuna for ~$14bn.

Analysts initially felt Cobenfy would command nearly $6bn per annum in peak revenues, but then, under BMY's stewardship, the drug failed to meet endpoints in a study as an adjunctive therapy in patients with inadequately controlled schizophrenia, and those peak revenue expectations were slashed to ~$2.6bn. The drug earned $10m of revenues in its first full quarter on the market, in Q3.

For me, Praxis must surely have placed itself in the M&A "shop window" in 2025 with a series of positive data readouts, and if BMY was prepared to pay $14bn to gain access to a single drug, what might a Big Pharma pay for three, plus an ASO platform? More than Praxis' current valuation of $6.2bn, seems likely.

Then again, does the subsequent failure of Cobenfy create doubts around biotechs touting breakthrough study wins in CNS indications? Investors are often searching for signs that a drug will secure approval, and be a commercial success, but indications that might be the case - i.e., Praxis strong study results - don't always play out that way in reality. For more information on the ulixacaltamide studies, this recent discussion transcript provides some intriguing insights.

My take, based on the fact there are still uncertainties in play, is that Praxis as an acquisition target is either worth substantially more than $6.2bn, or substantially less.

In 2025, to the end of Q3, the company reported a net loss of $214m, or $(9.97) per share, and a cash plus marketable securities position of ~$390m, so my guess would be that the company may try to raise funds imminently, to make sure it can fund all of its studies, plus two regulatory submissions, plus, ten months later, potentially, two commercial launches.

Would it be a struggle for Praxis, with <150 staff, to successfully secure approval for and launch two drugs into competitive markets, albeit markets where unmet need remains high? In my opinion, it would be, so can the Boston biotech attract a commercial partner, or secure an exit to a Big Pharma?

Reviewing the data, the alignment with the FDA, the MoAs, which are recognized and used in other commercial drugs - I believe Vertex' (VRTX) pain therapeutic Jounavx is also a sodium channel blocker - I see plenty of evidence to support a bull case. However, because it may take many years, and hundreds of millions of dollars spent on SG&A, to extract the full benefit from these drugs commercially, I am remaining on the sidelines, although I intend to monitor for dips in the share price, and provided they don't undermine my bull case, considering buying Praxis stock at that stage.

data.title: Veeco Instruments: Is It Worth Hanging Around, Or Is It Time To Go?
data.description:
The wafer fabrication equipment business is a brutal one. Not only do companies have to deal with stiff competition from entrenched majors like Applied Materials (AMAT), ASML (ASML), Lam Research (LRCX), and Tokyo Electron (OTCPK:TOELY), the sharp cyclicality of the industry and the need to constantly reinvest in R&D only adds to the pressure.

With that, those companies good enough to make it through a cycle or two often find they need to either pursue M&A to broaden their portfolio or accept an acquisition offer and become a part of somebody else’s growth story. Such is the case today with Veeco Instruments (VECO), which itself got to this point in its story in part through the successful acquisition of Ultratech back in 2017.

Up almost 30% since my last update, Veeco investors have to decide whether to hold on and accept shares in Axcelis Technologies (ACLS) in exchange for their Veeco shares, or sell ahead of the deal close and move to other ideas. Given the relative valuation and how I see the deal shaping up over time, as a Veeco shareholder I’m leaning toward making my exit.

Veeco, Like Many, Is Just Muddling Through Right Now

Like many other players in the WFE space, Veeco is muddling through a trough in orders that has been exacerbated by geopolitical squabbling over tariffs and technology transfer. Third quarter results reflected those challenges, with revenue down 10% year over year and flat sequentially, with both the Semiconductor and Compound Semiconductor businesses down both annually (down 5% and down 31%, respectively) and sequentially (down 5% and down 21%, respectively).

While Veeco has built a strong niche for itself in laser annealing equipment (a front-end process in semiconductor manufacturing that lowers silicon resistance and activates dopants to provide a sound foundation for the chip to be built), as well as ion beam etch/deposition, wet processing, and metal organic chemical vapor deposition (or MOCVD), the reality is that fabs simply aren’t ordering now, and the one area that’s comparatively healthier today (high-bandwidth memory) is one where the company is not yet as well-established.

Weaker revenue has had a negative impact on Veeco’s margins, but I do think it’s worth noting that this trough has been gentler than in the past – the company has remained profitable with a trough adjusted operating margin of 13.9% versus losses in past downcycles (like 2019) and free cash flow positive.

I believe this is a byproduct of a less severe cycle, but also management’s efforts to diversify the business and focus more on “mission critical” offerings for leading-edge processes that are somewhat less vulnerable to cyclical swings, including the company’s laser annealing for leading-edge nodes and repositioning the ion beam etch and MOCVD businesses for EUV litho mask blanks and GaN chip fabrication, respectively.

Gross margin declined about two points year over year and 70bp quarter over quarter in the third quarter (to 41.9%), while operating income fell 25% yoy with margin down about three points to 13.9% (sequentially the business was flat, with flat margins).

Veeco is likely looking at a couple more quarters of similar performance ahead of improving results later in 2026 as more evaluation programs convert to orders in annealing, overall order activity improves, and the hard drive business sees some recovery.

Axcelis Steps In Near The End Of The Trough

The “just wait for the recovery” thesis for Veeco got interrupted at the start of October when Axcelis and Veeco announced a friendly merger that will see Axcelis acquire Veeco for 0.3575 shares. Axcelis shareholders will own 58% of the combined company, and the Axcelis CEO will run the business after the deal. At the time of the deal, the offer represented a roughly 15% premium for Veeco shareholders, though ACLS shares have been weak since the announcement, falling about 15% while many large WFE companies have seen their shares rise by high-teens percentages or more.

To be blunt, I don’t really like this deal all that much.

Axcelis is a specialist in ion implantation, and while it has leverage to growth opportunities in silicon carbide (or SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) chips, the reality is that the bulk of the company’s business today is in older legacy nodes (management’s own quarterly presentations highlight this, with only 5% of shipments in Q3’25 classified as “advanced” logic or memory) and it doesn’t offer real exposure to leading-edge logic or HBM, as the company just doesn’t have much traction here.

While it is true that SiC (and GaN) are long-term growth opportunities, the recent downturn (particularly relative to the strength in leading-edge logic) highlights the risks of not having leading-edge exposure. Moreover, the company has been losing share to Applied Materials in ion implant and it’s unclear to me that Axcelis is really ideally-placed to take advantage of the SiC/GaN opportunities even when they do accelerate.

While management has talked about cross-selling (Taiwan Semi (TSM) is a long-time Veeco customer but doesn’t order from Axcelis) and offering integrated solutions combining front-end ion implant from ACLS and annealing from Veeco, I think there’s a real risk that customers simply won’t see the value in the ACLS offerings (though I don’t completely dismiss the idea that Veeco could at least get them through the door and get a sincere look at their tech).

I likewise have my doubts about the real synergies in the wide bandgap materials businesses (equipment for SiC, GaN, and GaN-on-SiC) and between ion implant and ion beam equipment. While dealing with a smaller number of suppliers can be beneficial, the reality is that leading-edge fabs are not going to be willing to sacrifice chip yields just for some incremental convenience.

I also feel like the strategies and philosophies at the two companies are different. Veeco has been actively repositioning itself toward a greater focus on equipment for leading-edge processes, including its nanosecond annealing for logic, ion beam deposition for HBM, wet processing for advanced packaging, and MOCVD for GaN-based power semiconductors.

Axcelis has much less exposure to leading-edge logic and memory and is stronger in legacy nodes. Moreover, while the company should be well-placed to leverage growth in SiC and GaN, this business has been slow to develop for Axcelis and there is low visibility across the SiC space now (I believe it’s a “when, not if” proposition, but that “when” could be longer than expected).

While I do think there will be some operating synergies with the two companies, it’ll take a couple of years for those synergies to emerge and I’m worried about the challenges involved in managing this expanded enterprise – Axcellis management has been focused on ion implantation for its history and will have to manage a much broader array of businesses and customers while also maintaining hard-won performance leadership in areas like annealing.

The Value

I realize this article comes off as quite negative on Axcelis, but that’s not really my intention or position on the company. Rather, I just think Axcelis isn’t particularly well-placed for this phase of the cycle and I think there is above-average integration and execution risk with this deal.

Looking at the growth I expect for both companies over the next couple of years, and adding in a couple of percentage points of sales synergy by 2027 and around 4% opex synergies, and then using my discounted cash flow and margin-driven EV/revenue and P/E approaches (including a 2.05x forward revenue multiple based on 18%+ adjusted EBITDA margins and a 17.5x multiple on earnings of $5.56), I get a value range of $77 to $97 for Axcelis and a corresponding $27.50 to $34.68 range for Veeco shares. With Veeco already in the middle of that range, even a relatively bullish outcome for the combined companies would only offer around 10% of upside while integration issues or weaker synergies would create more downside risk.

As far as upside risks to the thesis, basically consider the opposite to my major points. Perhaps Veeco will open doors to Axcelis and get customers to take a closer look at their offerings. Likewise, perhaps there will be more value and interest in integrated solutions combining ion implant and annealing. It’s also possible that the combination of the two companies’ respective wide bandgap capabilities (in implant and MOCVD) will lead to a more integrated solutions and/or sales synergies between existing customers, particularly given Axcelis’s existing presence in the SiC space, and Veeco’s early-stage efforts in GaN-on-SiC

The biggest driver in an upside scenario would be revenue growth, particularly if TSMC would start buying systems from Axcelis and if the combination accelerates interest Veeco’s MOCVD offerings for GaN. Strong incremental revenue growth would drive stronger margins and cash flows, perhaps supporting a fair value comfortably above $100.

The Bottom Line

The good and bad of modeling and valuation is that you can make the numbers tell you whatever you want if you torture them long enough. In other words, if you think that Axcelis should actually be worth more on the order of $95 to $110, more power to you, but I think there are still real questions about the strategic logic of this deal and the likelihood that the hoped-for benefits will materialize. In other words, Axcelis could be worth those higher numbers as a standalone but still lose value if the deal doesn’t work out.

I actually hope I’m wrong; as I said, I like Axcelis on its own merits and I think it is plausible that recoveries in SiC and growth in GaN will do meaningful good for the company’s long-term prospects. I’ve seen what happens when deals don’t produce the hoped-for benefits, though, and I see an elevated risk of that happening here. Shareholders don’t have to decide right away, as the deal isn’t expected to close until the second half of 2026, and that provides a little more time to assess Axcelis’s positioning in what should be a recovering market. That said, I’m still leaning heavily toward selling my Veeco shares and moving on to other ideas.

data.title: Roche May Be Riding A Bit Too High, But Remains Interesting
data.description:
November was good to Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) (OTCQX:RHHBF), as investor enthusiasm over programs like Gazyva in lupus, fenebrutinib in multiple sclerosis, and giredestrant in breast cancer fueled a 19% rally that certainly helped lessen the sting of a disappointing third quarter financial report that saw several key growth drivers slow noticeably (even if likely temporarily).

Roche shares have done well since my last update, with the ADRs up more than 35% against a roughly 22% positive move for the broader pharmaceutical space.

At this point, I’m a little more cautious on the shares. I do like management’s new approach to managing R&D and clinical development, and I believe it will increase productivity and returns over time. I also love the sheer scope and scale that the company offers. While internal R&D is usually the most cost-effective approach, Roche has the resources to buy its way into any therapeutic class it feels it needs.

The “but” is expectations and near-term drivers. A lot is riding on the upcoming persevERA study read-out for giredestrant, and disappointment here will likely be punished. Moreover, 2026 is otherwise looking thin for clinical catalysts, increasing the risk that the shares could lose institutional interest, particularly if/when rivals report strong clinical data for competing drugs. While still a good core long-term holding for patient investors, the risk/reward today may not be ideal for the next six months.

The Upcoming persevERA Read-Out Is A Big One

Giredestrant, Roche’s oral selective estrogen receptor degrader (or SERD), is one of the most significant compounds in the company’s late-stage pipeline. If clinical trials go well, this drug could generate over $7 billion in peak revenue, and possibly well over $10 billion if everything goes right, as it has the potential of becoming an “all comers” type of drug for the most common form of breast cancer.

Roche’s interim analysis of the Phase III lidERA study certainly helped stoke more enthusiasm. This study was designed to explore the use of the drug in a neoadjuvant setting for people with early-stage ER+ HER2- breast cancer, and the drug showed both a statistically significant and clinically meaningful improvement in disease-free survival versus endocrine therapy, as well as a clear positive trend in survival.

That’s certainly positive, but there are a few “buts” that need to be remembered. First, the control arm really doesn’t reflect the current standard of care (or at least not completely), as CDK4/6 drugs are now also in use. Thus, while the trial design ostensibly covers about 50% of the potential patient population, in “real-world use,” it may be more on the order of 20%.

With that in mind, there could be some risk when Roche reports the actual numbers (I believe they’ll be presenting at the SABCS conference that runs from Dec. 9 to Dec. 12). If giredesrant shows less than a 25% benefit to disease-free survival, it may well not be enough to satisfy rising expectations.

While adjuvant use is not a trivial opportunity (it could well exceed $10 billion/year in the 2030s), first-line use in metastatic disease is likely to be at least as large, and that’s what the perservERA study will address. Based upon early clinical work, it’s possible that giredestrant may work more broadly than competing SERDs from rivals like AstraZeneca (AZN) or Lilly (LLY), but clinical confirmation is needed. Given how the share price has responded since the positive lidERA press release, expectations are higher now, and Roche really needs this drug to come through with strong, clean data to support the momentum.

As Is Often The Case, The Pipeline Has Had Its Ups And Downs

This year (2025) has been a mixed one for Roche in terms of pipeline development. Looking at management’s list of key readouts from the time of the Q4’24 earnings call, astegolimab failed in its Phase III ARNASA COPD study, high-dose Ocrevus failed to show meaningful benefit, and results for vamikibart were mixed in uveitic macular edema (or UME), with one trial (MEERKAT) succeeding and one (SANDCAT) coming up short.

Clinical results for fenebrutinib were likewise mixed. While FENhance 2 showed significant reductions in relapses relative to Sanofi’s (SNY) Aubagio, the FENtrepid study only showed non-inferiority to Ocrevus in PPMS (though there was improvement starting from week 24). At this point, then, this is looking more like a “Ocrevus-extender” than a major step forward; not a bad drug, and certainly not a failure, but not meeting the most bullish hopes.

It hasn’t been all bad news, though. Giredestrant is looking good so far, and the ALLEGORY study of Gazyva/Gazyvaro in lupus (SLE, specifically) was a success, as was the INShore study in kids with idiopathic nephrotic syndrome. The company is also moving zosurabalpin into Phase III development for MDR bacterial infections, where it could become the first new drug for gram-negative bacteria in 50 years.

Looking at earlier-stage assets, a Phase Ib/IIa study of trontinemab showed significant plaque clearance (with 91% of patients becoming amyloid PET-negative), and the company is moving quickly into late-stage development for early symptomatic and pre-clinical Alzheimer’s indications. The company is also moving quickly into Phase III with NXT007, a new hemophilia A compound that holds promise for achieving hemostatic normalization (no bleeds) in a larger percentage of patients than Roche’s own drug Hemlibra or Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) Mim8.

Risks And Opportunities With A New Approach

As I discussed in my last article on Roche, I like how management has restructured its approach to R&D – culling drugs earlier in the process and using more rigorous criteria for advancing drugs, but also not dawdling when a drug seems meaningfully effective. I believe this has already upgraded the company’s later-stage pipeline assets, and I expect further improvements in R&D yield over the next five-plus years.

Roche has also been changing its approach regarding what it considers its addressable markets. Roche has long been a leader in relatively specialized fields like oncology, hematology, and neurology, but the company is now actively broadening its scope to more “general practice” medicines, including compounds for hypertension (its partnership with Alnylam (ALNY) on zilebesiran) and obesity.

Given the millions of people with these conditions, the revenue opportunities are huge. But then, so too is the scale of competition, as numerous major pharmas have followed Novo and Lilly (LLY) into this space over the last two years. These are also opportunities that will require a different marketing approach – while specialist conditions are more concentrated (fewer points of call), selling mass-market drugs requires a different approach, and it’s one where issues like pricing and access tend to be more significant.

By no means am I saying that Roche can’t succeed here; I’m simply pointing out that this is a notable change in strategy, and it will present some execution risks in the coming years.

The Outlook

Third-quarter results were not especially strong; while 7% constant-currency growth in the Pharma business and 2% growth in the Diagnostics business aren’t awful, both lines came in about 2% below expectations, and it was a noticeable slowdown from the double-digit Pharma growth in Q2. Likewise, while there are quarter-specific reasons why drugs like Ocrevus (up 6%) and Hemlibra (up 4%) decelerated, institutional investors often don’t care about the details and will bail out on stocks if they fear the main growth drivers are stalling.

The next few years are looking pretty solid for Roche. Relative to many large pharma companies, Roche has relatively little generics/biosimilar risk. I do see some competitive risks, though, including Novo’s Mim8 in hemophilia A (which is not yet approved, but has shown better bleed protection) and AstraZeneca/Daiichi Sankyo’s (OTCPK:DSNKY) Enhertu, which I do see potentially taking share from Roche’s Kadcyla and Perjeta over time.

I expect next year to be relatively similar to 2025 in terms of revenue growth (around 5%), and I’m looking for around 4% to 5% growth over the next five years, slowing toward 4% over the longer term. While major potential contributors like astegolimab and vamikibart may not look as robust as before, there are multiple drugs still in the pipeline with over $2 billion/year in peak revenue potential, with particular upside from names like giredestrant, trontinemab, zilebesiran, and NXT007 if trials go well.

On the margin side, I don’t expect too much turbulence, and I think the company will continue to produce EBITDA margins in the high 30% range and free cash flow margins in the low-to-mid-20% range on a pretty consistent basis over time. With that, I expect FCF growth a bit above revenue growth over the long term, but still in the 4%-5% range.

Between discounted cash flow and a growth-driven P/E approach (there’s a good historical correlation between five-year EPS growth and forward P/Es for pharma companies), I believe Roche is more or less at fair value today and priced for a long-term total annualized return in the 7% to 8% range.

The Bottom Line

I generally like to buy stocks like Roche when the prospective long-term return is in the double digits, but those opportunities don’t come around on any predictable schedule. With that, I think today’s prospective return is fine for investors with patience and a long-term orientation. For investors more interested in short-term performance, this may not be an ideal setup, as expectations for giredestrant have risen noticeably, and there may not be enough sentiment-moving readouts in 2026 apart from earlier-stage obesity assets to really stoke near-term momentum.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Bitcoin Selloff: What Liquidity & DXY Data Predict Next
data.description:
Within crypto, there are perma-bulls and perma-bears, but both are too emotional and dogmatic to consistently participate in Bitcoin’s epic swings. Bitcoin and crypto have consistently tested investors by doing the exact opposite of what the herd is expecting.

Last August, I had offered a rare explanation of how Bitcoin is sensitive to investor sentiment patterns and global liquidity trends, thus predicting the asset could be topping in an article entitled “Is Bitcoin’s Bull Run Nearing a Top? What the Herd Missed at $16,000 and is Missing Now”

In that article, I stated ...

...the system that helped us identify the $16,000 bottom in Bitcoin is telling a more complex story. Global liquidity appears to be stalling and setting up for a reversal. This is historically not good for Bitcoin and tends to coincide with major tops. This inflection point lines up with our Technical Analysis that has us in the final leg of the multi-year bull market.”

This is the core differentiator of our approach: we analyze Bitcoin not as a belief system, not as ideology, not as emotion — but as an asset driven by sentiment patterns, that also operates within a global liquidity machine.

Today, the buzz word is “liquidity” and how it’s draining from global markets, causing volatility in risk assets. This is not a surprise to our readers, as we have been discussing liquidity dynamics for months, as it is one of the few data-driven dynamics that can help predict Bitcoin’s swings.

However, looking forward, we could see more volatility before Bitcoin resumes its leadership to all-time highs. If you hold Bitcoin or have large unrealized gains, this is a crucial moment. The technical picture has shifted, increasing overall risk, with a scenario where we head lower. Our analysis breaks down the exact support levels that must hold, the scenario for a final push to new highs, and the path that would confirm a deeper downturn.

Global Liquidity and DXY: The Inverse Relationship Driving Bitcoin's Cycles

Since the market peak in late October, “liquidity” has become a buzzword, casually invoked as though its meaning were universally understood. Instead, liquidity is one of the most overused - and least understood - terms in financial markets.

Liquidity refers to the availability of capital in the system—specifically, how easily businesses, consumers, and financial institutions can obtain cash or credit. But when it comes to actually positioning a portfolio through different liquidity regimes, how this impacts risk-on assets often gets lost in translation.

In modern markets, liquidity is inseparable from debt dynamics. It is not the creation of new debt that dominates capital flows, but the ability to roll over existing obligations. In fact, three out of every four global financial transactions are related to debt refinancing, not expansion. Moreover, nearly 80% of global lending now requires collateral, typically in the form of high-quality, low-volatility assets like U.S. Treasuries.

This creates a framework where liquidity—and by extension, risk appetite—is dictated by how cheaply and easily borrowers can refinance without overextending their own balance sheets. The more capital that’s freed through this process, the more capital can rotate into risk-on assets such as Bitcoin.

A few key variables influence a country’s liquidity conditions:

  • Central bank policy
  • Fiscal spending
  • The Treasury General Account (TGA)
  • Federal Reserve repo operations
  • Broad equity market performance
  • Bond market volatility

Collectively, these forces determine whether capital and confidence flow into the system or are pulled out. An internal calculation we have created at the Tech Insider Network, which incorporates all of these factors, can be seen below. Domestic liquidity has broken through the 2022 low, driven predominantly by questionable FED policy, the Reverse Repo Operations at $0, and the Treasury General Account remaining elevated due to a shift in public financing policy.

Chart showing Domestic Liquidity (WALCL) levels falling below the critical 2022 lows, tracked via Reverse Repo, TGA Balance, and Fed’s balance sheet, signaling increased downside risk for risk assets and equities.

Even with Domestic liquidity in free fall, there is one factor that is the most important when discussing this topic - U.S. Dollar.

Roughly 64% of global debt is denominated in USD—which means foreign borrowers who accessed cheap U.S. capital must continue sourcing dollars to service that debt. When the dollar weakens relative to their local currencies, less local currency is needed to meet dollar obligations. This frees up capital that can chase higher-yielding risk assets, including Bitcoin.

This inverse relationship between the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) and Bitcoin has been both consistent and predictive across cycles:

Chart comparing Bitcoin's price to the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY), demonstrating that every major Bitcoin bull market occurs during a declining dollar, and bear markets coincide with a rising dollar.

In the above chart, three dynamics are evident:

  • Every major Bitcoin bull market occurred during a declining dollar.
  • Every significant Bitcoin bear market coincided with a rising dollar.
  • The steepness of the dollar’s trend often defines the magnitude of Bitcoin’s move in the opposite direction.

In August, we approached a critical inflection point. The Dollar Index had been in a clear downtrend since peaking in late September 2022—just weeks before Bitcoin bottomed. The most recent leg of this decline in the dollar shows a completed five-wave structure, typically the final phase of a correction before a reversal. Momentum is starting to shift upward, and a sustained break above $101 on the DXY would confirm a major low and the onset of a new dollar uptrend.

However, if we analyze the technical pattern in DXY, as long as DXY stays below $101, there is a setup where it drops to new lows. – likely targeting between $93 - $89.

Technical Analysis of the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) showing potential final 5th wave drop toward $93-$89. The chart highlights the critical $101 resistance level and uses momentum indicators to predict an imminent move lower, increasing global liquidity and likely pushing Bitcoin higher.

Note how the current bounce is not a direct move higher. It is a messy, and overlapping push higher, which is characteristic of corrections within a larger trend – in this case, pointing lower. Furthermore, the momentum indicators point to a rare overbought condition, while also showing that momentum is fading the higher we go. This is what we see at the end of swings, suggesting a move lower is imminent.

If DXY fails under $101, and the following drop is a more vertical and aggressive drop lower, then we could see an extended drop in DXY to new lows, which would increase global liquidity, and by extension, should push Bitcoin higher.

Having correctly called the U.S. Dollar Index low earlier this year, we noted that, “Until DXY can break above $101, it can still make another low, which will further support higher prices in Bitcoin. The takeaway here is to note that the U.S. dollar is closer to a major low than most think. While it can extend further, and likely will, once we get evidence of a major trend reversal, this should line up with a topping process in Bitcoin. Until then, we can and should see Bitcoin continue an upward trajectory.

Bitcoin Price Drivers: Why Sentiment and Technical Analysis Beat Fundamentals

Bitcoin is unlike stocks. There are no earnings reports, no 10-Ks, no revenue models, no management teams to evaluate, and no real competition—despite occasional claims that alternatives like Ethereum could challenge it. The reality is simple: Bitcoin has already established itself as the definitive digital store of value.

Thematic investing also suggests that the positive news developments are tailwinds for Bitcoin that should support higher prices from here. In fact, we are hearing the same today – A favorable administration that is supporting Bitcoin, as well as a strategic Bitcoin reserve being established. These are both bullish narratives for Bitcoin, which should logically support higher prices. However, if we look at history, Bitcoin has an uncanny inclination to do the opposite of what the news-based narrative at the time suggests. In other words, it likes to top on bullish news and bottom on bearish news.

Chart comparing Bitcoin Price to major news events, demonstrating that movements are driven by market psychology and technical analysis patterns—like the five-wave pattern—rather than fundamentals or bullish narratives.

So, if narratives do not seem to affect price, what actually drives its price? One of the major drivers of crypto is sentiment, which can only be measured through the lens of technical analysis.

Sentiment is simply analyzing herd mentality, which manifests in repeatable patterns. It is a powerful force in the markets. When you funnel human consciousness into a quantifiable system like public markets, strange patterns emerge and they emerge time and time again on all timeframes, on all markets and going back throughout history. Patterns like triangle, bull flags, bear flags, head and shoulder patterns, cup and handle patterns, are very common technical analysis patterns that gauge where the herd is likely moving next.

The most important pattern within technical analysis is the five wave pattern, which we have discussed in the clip below. It underpins all of this and is what marks a trend.

The fact that a clear 5 wave uptrend has completed off the 2022 low, with the final push moving higher on decelerating volume, had us quite concerned. The most likely path forward has been higher, which I presented in our August report - yet this has now been invalidated, forcing me to create a more unconventional path higher. While this path is possible, it is not probable, based on the technical outlook alone.

The chart explaining Bitcoin's 2022 Bull Cycle has completed a full Five-Wave Pattern, with weakening Volume and RSI trends signaling increased market risk. The breaking of key support levels below $74,440 suggests a major warning for the bulls.

However, if any break lower can hold over $74,440, then this path is valid and worth monitoring, especially if we see DXY break lower. We could even see this drop push toward $67,000 in an extended move. However, below $67,000 and there is no path higher, before seeing an extended bear cycle take us back into the $40,000 region or lower.

What is also concerning can be seen in the volume and momentum patterns above. Note how volume has always expanded as price went higher, then contracted as price corrected. This is a healthy trend and one we have used to time buys along the way.

However, since the April low in 2025, we have reversed this trend. Volume has been decelerating as price went higher, and now it is expanding as price moves lower. This is not a healthy trend and is signaling that sellers are starting to show up in force.

This is further backed by the RSI breaking through its bull market support. Note how each correction found a low when the RSI bottomed out around 33. This support has held throughout the bull cycle. However, it recently broke this support, which is not what we typically see in bull cycles.

We are not only seeing critical supports break in Bitcoin, but the volume and momentum trends are shifting in real time. Technicals alone are flashing big warnings for the bulls, which has us in a much more defensive posture than any time during this bull cycle.

Regarding the bull scenario, we would need to see a vertical push over $114,000 before the odds start shifting in that direction. If this happens, we will continue our game plan to trade Bitcoin in its final swings of the epic bull cycle that started in 2022.

On-Chain Analysis

We’ve consistently relied on WealthUmbrella’s top-tier On-Chain Analysis throughout this cycle. Their model flashed a buy alert in December 2022, around the same time our own system showed a major opportunity in Bitcoin.

Since the new bull cycle in Bitcoin started in December of 2022, we are seeing the largest price drop within this cycle, which is currently greater than -35%. Strangely, and unlike any period of notable volatility we have experienced in the last 3 years, the on-chain health of Bitcoin simply does not warrant the size of this drawdown.

While we are seeing a healthy on-chain ecosystem, what is driving the current period of volatility is ETF selling. We were expecting this dynamic at some point, because the hype around the BTC spot ETF created an unusually tight correlation between Bitcoin and the stock market. In that environment, Bitcoin was unlikely to thrive while market breadth was deteriorating and signaling a risk-off shift.

This new dynamic has linked Bitcoin to the state of the equity market, which could see another leg lower. However, our cyclical indicators suggest that we are likely not experiencing a cyclical top, and any further volatility could be considered a mini bear phase, within a larger bull cycle, like we experienced in 2013 and 2021.

For example, new non-zero-balance addresses remains quite healthy. People are still joining the network at a steady rate.

On-Chain Analysis chart showing Bitcoin's network adoption remains healthy and strong, evidenced by a steady increase in new non-zero-balance addresses despite the recent -37% price drop in the bull cycle.

Furthermore, this is happening while the sentiment of people who hold Bitcoin as an actual crypto asset on the blockchain remains far from bearish. Not only is the influx of new participants on the blockchain still very healthy, but whales have also been aggressively buying the dips. If we look at holders controlling more than one hundredth of the total supply (around 200,000 BTC), their balances have actually increased since the end of October.

On-Chain Chart illustrating significant Bitcoin Whale accumulation (approx. 110,000 BTC) during the recent price drop, contrasting underlying network strength with masking ETF outflows and signaling seller exhaustion.

In fact, their accumulation — roughly 110,000 BTC — is almost identical to what they accumulated during the “Tariff correction” earlier this year (about 120,000 BTC). These are not signs of desperation, and it’s entirely possible that the scenario where whales ultimately come out on top of this correction will play out once again.

The issue is that ETF outflows are masking this underlying strength. That being said, ETF balance sheets have already shrunk by about 4% in native units (from 1.362 million BTC to 1.307 million BTC), on top of a decline of roughly 36% in value at the worst point. Not only do we expect the stock market to rebound somewhat, but Friday’s flush happened on noticeably lower BTC ETF outflows, which is a classic pattern of seller exhaustion.

Chart of Bitcoin Spot ETF Net Inflow/Outflow shows recent volatility driven by ETF selling, with a notable slowdown in outflows signaling potential seller exhaustion and a shift away from a full cycle top.

Now, as we sit nearly $10k above the recent low, it’s clear that we are at least in a bounce. The question is whether this is just a bounce or the beginning of a renewed bullish trend?

According to our analysis, while ETF dynamics are creating a new risk in our models, we are simply not seeing the type of signals that coincides with a cycle top. We created four cycle top indicators, which monitors different layers of the Bitcoin ecosystem. None of these indicators have reached levels that is consistent with a true euphoric/overbought top consistent with prior major tops.

Chart showing Bitcoin Cycle Top Indicators remain below euphoric/overbought levels, suggesting the current volatility is due to ETF selling and not consistent with a final cyclical Bitcoin top consistent with the end of bull cycles in Bitcoin.

The current correction, as shown above, has a unique dynamic, which is the result of ETF adoption. The volatility we are experiencing is being driven mostly by ETF selling and is a stark reminder of the risks associated with Bitcoin. Even in a neutral on-chain environment — not a bearish one — ETFs selling just 4% of their holdings triggered a 35% drop in price. This highlights how illiquid the market has been over the last few months. It’s something that has concerned us since 2022, when the proportion of long-term holders on the blockchain became extraordinarily high (the share of coins unmoved for a year peaked at 71% at the end of 2023). Bitcoin’s strength back then was that nobody was selling, unlike in 2017 when everyone was instead eager to buy. ETFs brought back a bit of that demand dynamic, but it became clear that unless euphoria returns on-chain, Bitcoin will remain tightly correlated to stock-market risk-off movements and may suffer from liquidity shortages during equity corrections. In our view, the long-term risk of holding Bitcoin will diminish once the balance between ETF-driven buying and on-chain buying normalizes, and once Bitcoin’s strength relies less on a pure hodler mindset and more on a healthy, rotating, and liquid market.

In Conclusion

Bitcoin is undeniably the most lucrative asset in market history with an astronomical return of over 100,000%. Even if you missed day one, there have been many opportunities to participate along the way. This is where our firm has excelled as we have a strong track record of trimming near local tops and loading back up at lower prices. This can significantly shift total return.

Beginning in August, our tone changed. We began to warn that the risk in Bitcoin was rising, even though the same talking heads that missed the lows were telling us the rally was just beginning.

Please note: The Tech Insider Network conducts research and draws conclusions for the Fund’s positions. We then share that information with our readers. This is not a guarantee of a stock’s performance. Please consult your personal financial advisor before buying any stock in the companies mentioned in this analysis.

data.title: Box Q3: Limited Alpha Ahead
data.description:
Since my last Box (BOX) analysis, the stock has lost 3% in price; the company just reported Q3 earnings, which is the focus of this report. My price target for BOX is $36, and the current price is $31.80, so the implied upside is 13.2%. As this is likely only marginal alpha over the S&P 500 (SPY), my rating is an objective Hold.

Q3 Earnings

Box's Q3 results won't excite elite allocators seeking to "break the market," but to management's credit, the business is operating well and is investable. Cash flows appear durable, capital discipline is strong, and there's a meaningful transformation from cloud storage to AI-driven content infrastructure to support operational trajectory and thematic sentiment.

For the quarter, revenue was up 9% year-over-year, while gross margin was essentially flat versus a year ago. Operating margin was down 20 bps from a year ago, and net income decreased marginally from $12.9 million to $12.1 million. Diluted EPS was flat versus last year. Top-line and operating metrics are grinding higher, but GAAP EPS looks soft because of tax normalization and ongoing investment in R&D and go-to-market.

The business's stability was exemplified by its remaining performance obligations being up 18% year-over-year, with management expecting to recognize about 55% of RPO in the next 12 months and ~80% within 24 months. In the earnings call, management also highlighted that billings grew ~12%, faster than revenue, and net retention reached ~104%—an indication that customers are expanding spend meaningfully. Non-GAAP free cash flow was up 7% year-over-year for the quarter.

Given that the company operates with a high gross margin of about 80%, is cash generative, and is slowly expanding its operating leverage, it's difficult to see this as a risky investment (especially when we analyze the valuation below).

Management has also made it clear that it believes the company's stock is cheap relative to intrinsic value, with Q3 share repurchases at 2.4 million with a weighted average price of $32.11 (totaling $76.8 million). Nine-month repurchases are at 5.2 million shares at $166.6 million, and remaining authorization as of Q3-end is $35.5 million.

Further Call Analysis

Management was honed-in on AI in the earnings call, and made it clear that Box is now a "leader in Intelligent Content Management," not just cloud storage. The AI layer of the stack now includes Box AI for Metadata, Box AI Studio, Box Extract, Box Automate, and Box AI Agents/Extract Agents, which are all designed to sit on top of Box content and tie into third-party LLMs.

Management also spoke on new and expanded partnerships, which include a multi-year AI collaboration with AWS and a strategic partnership with Tata Consultancy Services. This operating vision of intelligent content management built on integration for holistic efficiency is obviously effective from first principles—though it can't be said to be a macroculture-defining technology stack, it is certainly one that has broad-based utility, and hence long-term value creation is likely ahead of it.

From what I've gleaned, management clearly knows the market narrative has to move beyond Box being "commodity storage," and they are clearly willing to take restructuring charges now ($0.2 million in charges this quarter and $8.3 million year-to-date) to fund higher-value growth areas. Moreover, they're holding the non-GAAP margin line while they do it, which shows financial-operational competence. Moreover, I have heard Aaron Levie, Box's CEO, speak many times, and I appreciate his demeanor and sense of forward direction.

The company has decisively shifted to a subscription-first model, which is good for both scalability and predictability of the core business, and the gross margin around 80% is elite for an enterprise-software company. RPO growth consistently outpacing revenue growth is also a sure sign that longer contract durations and multi-year enterprise commitments can sustain the valuation in the medium to long term. Box is also cleverly avoiding the capex arms race of model training while still monetizing integration.

Valuation

We began with the price chart above, and this is an apt outline of where the price is headed given the fundamental factors we have discussed in this thesis. Looking further at the price-to-fundamentals also shows de facto fair value. The forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is roughly in-line with the sector median, and the forward price-to-cash-flow ratio actually shows undervaluation, at -40% below the sector. Stock-based compensation is currently high ($232.1m TTM, $219m FY25, $198.8m FY24, etc.), so cash flow naturally looks stronger in the valuation analysis, given TTM net income of $227.7m.

As indicated in my technical analysis at the beginning of this report, I am targeting a $36 stock price in 12 months, based on my intuitive approach to trend-line assessment based on holistic fundamental and operational factors as evidenced above. The indicated 12-month return from my approach is 13.2%, a worthy Hold or risk-off Buy.

Risks

Evidently, Box sits in a crowded arena where Microsoft (MSFT) 365, Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) Workspace, and others can bundle "good enough" content and collaboration into broader suites—Box's own 10-K states that a failure to sustain renewals, seat expansion, and upgrades into higher-tier plans would pressure growth and profitability.

Strategically, Box's multi-year collaboration with AWS appears shrewd, but in the medium to long term, Box's customers may not pay materially more for these features, especially when they are already being sold AI inside of Microsoft and Google estates.

The capital structure and share dilution are also downside variables that are sometimes ignored by FCF proponents. Stock-based compensation of $219 million in FY25 on $1.09 billion of revenue is an SBC margin of about 20%. Therefore, a large part of the repurchase program is about offsetting issuance rather than shrinking the float for value creation.

Finally, it's worth remembering that this is not an elite alpha position—though I am happy to analyze the company and commend management for its operating strengths (at the end of the day, a successful business does not have to achieve, nor seek, alpha—only funds and professional traders/investors do).

Conclusion: Hold

I consider Box to be well-run, and it's cash-generative, so it's relatively low-risk. Management's strategy in and of itself creates a margin of safety with a valuation that is de facto fair, or by some standards, cheap. My $36/share 12-month price target is conservative, but likely, and it indicates only modest upside roughly in line with the S&P 500. This is a name to add to watchlists and potentially accumulate on weakness for a sentiment-value trade, but for now it's a Hold in my book.

data.title: Wall Street Roundup: Netflix Buying Warner Brothers
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Netflix (NFLX) is buying Warner Brothers (0:20). Dollar General (DG), Salesforce (CRM) earnings (6:40). December Fed meeting (12:30). Meta chooses AI over metaverse (19:00).

Transcript

Rena Sherbill: Brian Stewart, our Director of news at Seeking Alpha. Welcome to December, 2025.

Brian Stewart: Great to be here.

Rena Sherbill: Great to have you talk to us. What's top of mind for you this week?

Brian Stewart: Well, we're past Thanksgiving. There's snow on the grounds. Christmas is a couple weeks away, so we're in a dead spot in terms of catalysts for the market. A lot of the companies go into hibernation at this point, not wanting to make too much noise before the end of the year while they have the staff shortages that come along with the holidays.

Meanwhile, trading desks are also pretty light at this time of year, so you see volume go down. So you kind of have to take whatever news comes your way and incorporate that into your investment thesis as best you can.

Today we got the news that Netflix (NFLX) is buying Warner Brothers (WBD) for $72 billion. It's the latest in the long string of the soap opera that is the Warner history in 2018. It was bought by AT&T and then 2022 merged with Discovery and now being purchased by Netflix.

I was joking with Kim Khan that is kind of the Liz Taylor of companies, you know, just gets married eight times and keeps it keeps on going. So we'll see what happens from here.

If you're bullish on the deal gives Netflix an edge in the streaming wars. They're adding HBO Max kind of remains to be seen how they're going to handle that, whether they're going to have the the two apps going simultaneously or whether they're going to try and integrate the HBO content into Netflix proper.

But I've seen some analysts say that this is the catalyst that Netflix needs to to jump into the big leagues, become a one trillion dollar company and kind of go from there.

If you're bearish, this deal involves Netflix taking on more debt. Warner has been something of a cursed asset thus far, keeps getting kind of passed around. So it kind of depends on your perspective as this moves forward.

Rena Sherbill: Certainly fits in with the theme that we've been talking about. I think since the origin of this show, know, things are changing. Netflix buying Warner Brothers seems like a cementing of the new guard, would you say?

Brian Stewart: Yeah, it's interesting because if you look at Warner, the entire history of it, the other famous deal in the Warner canon, if you look at the mythos of the history of Warner is the AOL Time Warner deal, which was another, like you're saying, era defining deal.

So it's kind of interesting that here we are, what, 25 years later, something like that, that we're seeing another Warner being involved in another deal that you could peg as a sign of the times. So yeah, it's kind of interesting that history played out similarly that way.

Rena Sherbill: What would you give that AOL Time Warner deal and what would you give this deal if you had to grade it?

Brian Stewart: Historically, people have kind of clowned on the AOL Time Warner deal. So, you know, like consensus is that's an F. I actually have a different perspective on it.

AOL took its inflated stock price and turned it into real asset by buying Time Warner. So if you look at it from a Time Warner perspective, that that probably wasn't the best merger. you know, if you look at the stock price before and after of the combined company, it doesn't look too good if you're AOL and you turned inflated currency into a real asset that has value, so it was probably the way you should do it.

It's hard to obviously, in the exact wake of the deal, it's hard to judge. I mean, it's much easier with 25 years of hindsight to judge it. But I would say it's certainly a move by Netflix to cement its place in a sort of content as king world, it's adding things like The Sopranos and The Wire and a lot of highly anticipated, highly rewatched shows. So if you're building sort of a solid library that's going to keep people around for a while, I think that's smart.

It's more a prestige play than your typical Netflix. When I think of Netflix, I think of things like Love is Blind and comedy specials and things like that at this point. So if you kind of combine this as sort of like a legacy purchase with Netflix's other strategy moves of adding live events, they've been doing boxing and other things like that, I think it's certainly an interesting play.

Especially in a world where your competitor is Disney (DIS) and Disney has its own studio that's feeding Marvel products and Star Wars products and things into its streaming service.

I think this gives Netflix a little sturdier place to compete. Whether or not it's going to work out is really going to kind of depend on how well it can build that content pipeline, if it can keep finding the next Game of Thrones, the next Sopranos.

Rena Sherbill: Love is Blind presents the new Harry Potter.

Brian Stewart: Well, I mean, there was a lot of controversy about HBO's strategy of turning HBO into, you go on the HBO Max app, there's a lot of House Hunters type shows and things like that. And people were saying that was diluting the brand, which is sort of heavy prestige.

And so it'd be interesting to see how Netflix handles that, if they're going to have some sort of hard division between the kind of prestige stuff that you find in sort of the legacy HBO brand versus the more just turn on the TV and stay on your phone kind of stuff that you find elsewhere.

I think it'll be interesting how they handle it. Disney, they have everything on the Disney Plus app, but then they have the different channels for things like Marvel and Star Wars and things. So it'll be interesting to see if there's tiles on Netflix along the same lines.

Rena Sherbill: Yes, yes, lots of interesting moments and notes to be aware of and to see developing along the way. Very interesting. So many levels, so many different things, so many different things to look at as they evolve and develop this one of them, certainly.

What else you got for us? We saw some earnings this week, Dollar General (DG), Salesforce (CRM).

Brian Stewart: Like I said, it's a sparse week. Next week is sparse as well in terms of sort of marquee kind of companies. think Salesforce and Dollar General are good and just sort of like taking a temperature of some of the topics we've been talking about. Dollar General popped 14 % after it reported its earnings. It's also up another 5 % today. So it's building on those gains.

There's no pullback in the wake of that jump. It's up 37% from November 6th. you're seeing the company top expectations, raises outlook, benefited from higher traffic and a margin recovery. The general thesis coming out of the earnings report is that inflation fatigue is driving consumers into the lower price bracket.

Places like Dollar General and Dollar Tree (DLTR) and Walmart (WMT), Walmart we already saw was benefiting from this. So you're seeing people kind of search for deals. This is an interesting data point headed into the holiday season.

So far, it seems like you have a deal conscious consumer, especially in a situation where the labor market is still a little wonky. We don't quite know what's happening there. So I think this is just a sign that the consumer is worried about the bottom line, just sort of the affordability question that's been dogging politics is now also part of the economic equation.

And then we switched to Salesforce. Stock was up about 4% on its earnings. Again, it's up another 5% today. So again, you're seeing sort of a building response. The company has strong results and guidance. One of the things that a lot of the analysts coming out of it flagged was its AgentForce product was seen as a catalyst. That's an AI powered product.

It had AR growth of 114%. If you're, you're bearish on the stock, which a lot of people have been so far this year, the ARR for its agent force and data 360 products, which are the sort of AI package are currently at $1.4 billion. That's compared to a total revenue of 41.5 billion in 2026.

So while you're seeing a lot of AI growth from the company lately, it's not really a huge percentage of its bottom line. Its legacy business is still the largest by far. It's the AI at this point is sort of a drop in the bucket compared to the overall revenue situation.

And so if you're a shareholder, what you want to see is that sort of catch up for the AI to sort of take over because of the investment the company has made. So far this year, Salesforce has been the second worst performer in the Dow, sort of the 30 components. It's the one that's shown the second worst performance is down about 22 % year to date.

That's even after the gains that it made post earnings, only UnitedHealth (UNH) has had a worse performance so far in the Dow. So the company's been lagging behind, it's had trouble just sort of implementing this AI strategy.

And I think if you're looking at this from a general perspective, it's interesting to look at kind of the consumer side of the AI situation. So you have companies like Nvidia (NVDA), which are providing the chips, and then you have companies like Meta (META) and Apple (AAPL) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) who are trying to implement the AI strategies on the front lines.

Salesforce falls into that category. And so you're seeing companies struggle to implement the strategy, turn that investment into revenue. And you're seeing a little bit of a turnaround, at least in terms of investor consensus about Salesforce after this recent earnings that maybe that AI revenue is starting to show the turnaround that they wanted to have.

And the question is can this keep up? We'll have to wait till next quarter to know if the growth rates accelerate from here. But that's certainly what investors are betting on at this point.

Rena Sherbill: Before we hopped on this call, I saw a piece come out of your news team saying that nine out of 10 companies have delivered EPS wins this week, among them CrowdStrike (CRWD). Ulta Beauty (ULTA) was another one that crushed expectations. HP Enterprise (HPE), to your point about the tech space, theirs was a bit more mixed.

Anything to say and also maybe as it pertains to next week's earnings coming up, anything to say about the earnings picture in general and what that may mean or what you may be calling from that?

Brian Stewart: If we look ahead to the next week, have Oracle (ORCL) and Adobe (ADBE). Oracle especially has made a lot of big bets on the AI space.

So you're talking about companies like CrowdStrike and you're talking about Salesforce. I mean, these are the kinds of companies that are trying to implement the AI strategy in, and I said consumer, I realized that Salesforce is sort of B2B business, but I mean like a customer focused sort of process.

Oracle I think isn't the next data point on that progression the extent to which the AI strategy, the investment that these companies are making is paying off. Adobe is another one that's implementing AI processes and functionality in its products and how much it can use those to generate more income.

Also next week you have Toll Brothers (TOL), which is a home builder. So that's a part of the economy that we haven't really gotten a key earnings report from in a little bit. So that'll be kind of interesting along those lines.

And then you also have Costco (COST) coming next week. So that plays into the retail sector, the Dollar General sector, because Costco is another lower price option. So we can see whether that confirms the idea that the customers are starting to kind of move down the pricing ladder a little bit.

Rena Sherbill: As it pertains to the broader economic picture, we had Steve Cress on Investing Experts this week and he was talking about the volatility in terms of what's gonna happen at the December meeting. It's like 20% jumps up and down between what investors are expecting to happen, whether or not there's gonna be a rate cut.

What are you seeing? What are you feeling? What are you thinking as it pertains to the Fed and the economy and monetary policy?

Brian Stewart: The expectations for this upcoming Fed meeting have been crazy. There's probably a better way to describe it, but I don't know that I can remember a meeting where the perceptions about it have changed as quickly and dramatically as it has.

As I checked going into this discussion, there was an 87% chance that there was going to get a rate cut at next week's meeting that had been as low as about 30% as of mid-November, heading into the Thanksgiving week, was around 30%. So basically, we're pretty sure that there's not going to be a cut, a few weeks later, we're pretty sure there is going to be cut.

That's a pretty broad jump. Meanwhile, it was at 90% in late October. So you see it flopping around a lot. So as it stands, the general consensus is pretty solid in favor of there's going to be a rate cut at the next meeting. But as you point out, and as I'm sure Steve gave an excellent description of it's hard to say whether that's going to hold. mean, we're in the point where we're in the blackout period for for Fed speak.

There's not going to be any new meaningful data between now and when the Fed decision comes out. So, I think there's a low probability chance that we're going to see a lot of big swings from from now until then, but that doesn't mean that the Fed's going to cooperate. There's a lot of dissension within the Fed. There's there's been a lot of sort of back and forth.

Different voting members of the policy setting committee have very strong opinions whether or not to cut or not. So we're to have to see how that plays out in the meeting. beyond that, what the projections are going forward, like the dot plot is also going to come out next week. And so that's going to give us a good idea of what people see for 2026.

Rena Sherbill: Anything else to say data wise?

Brian Stewart: The only thing to note is that we're still catching up from the government shutdown. So we had the PCE data for September come out, come out today. It was pretty much in line with what people expected. 2.8 % annual rate of inflation, which is where it's been for a while, just sort of slightly below 3%. The jobs data today should have been a jobs day under normal circumstances, but,

We're not going to get the next jobs report till the middle of the month. So we're still kind of getting back to normal in terms of that data, which, you know, it's starting to clarify the economic situation a little bit, but we're still flying blind, kind of flying by our instruments in a fog at this point. So we're still going to have to see how it goes.
